description.task: Simulate Spring Java-based configuration using pure Java (no Spring). Create a configuration class that acts as a factory for all application beans, mimicking @Configuration and @Bean annotations.
instructions: Complete AppConfig.dataService() to return new DataService("PostgreSQL").
Complete AppConfig.cacheService() to call dataService() and pass result to CacheService.
In Main, create AppConfig, get CacheService, and call getCached().
Also get DataService and call getData() directly.
description.hint: In Spring, @Configuration classes are special — Spring intercepts @Bean method calls to ensure beans are singletons. This exercise simulates that pattern.
description.expectedOutput:
Data from PostgreSQL
[CACHED] Data from PostgreSQL

description.task: Implement a component-scanning mechanism using Java Reflection that discovers classes with a custom @Component annotation and registers them as beans.
instructions: In scan(), check each class for @Component. If present, instantiate using no-arg constructor and store using annotation value() as key.
In Main, scan InventoryService, ShippingService, HelperUtil.
Retrieve InventoryService and call checkStock('Laptop'), retrieve ShippingService and call ship('Laptop').
description.hint: Spring's @ComponentScan does exactly this — it scans packages, finds @Component/@Service/@Repository annotated classes, and registers them.
description.expectedOutput:
Registered beans: [inventoryService, shippingService]
Laptop: In Stock
Shipping: Laptop

description.task: Implement an environment profiles system in Java that loads different configurations based on the active profile (dev, staging, prod).
description.context: Spring Profiles allow different beans and configurations per environment. This is essential for applications deployed to multiple environments.
instructions: Implement getConfig() with a switch/if-else for profiles 'dev', 'staging', 'prod', and throw IllegalArgumentException for unknown profiles.
In Main, print the config for all three profiles.
description.hint: In Spring, use @Profile('dev') on @Bean methods or @Component classes to activate them only in specific environments, controlled via spring.profiles.active.
description.expectedOutput:
Dev: DB[url=jdbc:h2:mem:devdb, user=devuser, poolSize=5]
Staging: DB[url=jdbc:postgresql://staging-db/app, user=stageuser, poolSize=10]
Prod: DB[url=jdbc:postgresql://prod-db/app, user=produser, poolSize=50]
